planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Dragon fruit is an epiphytic cactus that needs a sturdy support structure — a wooden post 6–8 feet tall is typical for commercial production; in home gardens it can also be grown on a fence or trellis. The plant blooms at night (flowers open around 8 PM and close by dawn), requiring night-time hand pollination to guarantee fruit set — gently transfer pollen with a brush or cotton swab between flowers of the same plant (self-fertile variety) or between plants. In zone 9 protect from any frost — even brief exposure to freezing temperatures can damage the succulent stems. In the warmest zone 9 locations, established plants may survive mild winters with heavy mulching of the root zone.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil — dragon fruit is an epiphytic cactus requiring extremely well-drained soil and excellent air circulation; American Beauty is one of the most popular self-fertile dragon fruit cultivars, producing large (0.5–2 pound), oval fruits with vivid pink-red skin, prominent green-tipped scales, and brilliant magenta-red flesh with small, soft black seeds — one of the most visually stunning and nutritionally distinctive tropical fruits available. The intense magenta-red color of the flesh is caused by betacyanin pigments (similar to those in beets) that transfer vivid color to smoothies, drinks, yogurt, and desserts that incorporate the fruit, creating a dramatically colored culinary presentation that has made dragon fruit one of the most popular colorful ingredients in contemporary food photography and high-end food service. As an epiphytic cactus, dragon fruit requires a different cultural approach from conventional fruit trees — treating it more like a succulent with periodic deep watering rather than regular irrigation, and providing structural support for the long, three-ribbed, succulent stems that climb by aerial roots. Zone 9 gardeners in the warmest, most frost-free locations can grow dragon fruit successfully in the ground; others should use large containers that can be moved to frost-free locations during cold spells.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dragon Fruit Production in Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Dragon Fruit for South Texas
California Rare Fruit Growers — Hylocereus and Selenicereus Varieties

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–April. Guava is frost-sensitive — temperatures below 27°F can kill established trees, though mature trees may resprout from the roots after moderate freezes. In zone 9a plant in the warmest, most frost-protected microclimate (south or west wall exposure with thermal mass). Trees typically begin bearing fruit within 2–3 years of planting and produce two crops per year in zone 9 — spring and fall. Psidium guajava is highly invasive in Hawaii and other Pacific island ecosystems; confirm planting is appropriate for your location.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — guava is adaptable to a wide range of soils and is drought tolerant once established; Tropical White is a white-fleshed guava cultivar producing medium-sized, round to oval fruits with pale green to yellow skin at full ripeness and cream-white to pale yellow flesh with a mild, sweet, aromatic flavor and a texture ranging from smooth to granular depending on harvest timing. The flavor of white-fleshed guava is milder and less intensely tropical than pink-fleshed varieties — appealing to consumers who find the strong, musky, distinctive guava flavor of red-fleshed types overpowering. Common guava (Psidium guajava) is one of the most productive and easily grown tropical fruit trees in zone 9 — adapting to a wide range of soils, producing fruit year-round, and growing rapidly from a young tree to full bearing size. The highly aromatic fruit — which contains more vitamin C per gram than citrus — is consumed fresh, in juice, paste (guava paste is a classic Latin American confection), jam, and desserts across the tropical world. In zone 9 the combination of easy culture, fast production, and highly aromatic fruit that cannot be found in commercial distribution makes guava one of the most rewarding tropical fruit trees available.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Guava Production in Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Guava for South Texas Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Fruit for Gulf South Zone 9

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–April. Hymenocallis x festalis bulbs can remain in the ground year-round in zone 9 if given winter protection with heavy mulch; in colder zones dig and store bulbs above 40°F during winter. The white, fragrant flowers appear in late spring and early summer (May–July in zone 9) on tall scapes — each scape bears 3–5 flowers. The fragrance is sweetly powerful in the evening. Plants in zone 9 produce increasing numbers of flowering scapes each year as the bulbs multiply — well-established clumps can produce 20–30 flower scapes in a single season. The bulb is toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil — Peruvian Daffodil performs particularly well in partial shade in zone 9 where afternoon shade reduces the stress of peak summer heat during the blooming period; Peruvian Daffodil (Hymenocallis x festalis) is a spider lily hybrid of South American origin producing among the most elegant and exotic-looking white flowers of any summer bulb — each pure white flower has a large, central, funnel-shaped cup (corona) surrounded by six long, narrow, reflexed white petals that extend outward like spider legs, creating a dramatic, intricate structure entirely different from any conventional lily or daffodil form. The powerful sweet fragrance, strongest in the evening, is one of the most intensely pleasant of any summer-blooming flower — filling a garden area with a rich, sweet, tropical scent that attracts sphinx moths (hawkmoths) as pollinators. The combination of exotic flower structure, intense fragrance, white color (which reflects beautifully in the dusk and evening garden), and adaptability to partial shade makes Hymenocallis one of the most distinctive and memorable summer bulbs available for zone 9 gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hymenocallis Bulbs for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Spider Lily Relatives for Gulf South
North Carolina State University Extension — Peruvian Daffodil Cultivation

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall (September–November in zone 9); fall planting allows root establishment before the spring bloom season. Charjoy's Jan is a registered Louisiana iris cultivar from the Charjoy series developed by hybridizer Charles Chapman. Louisiana iris rhizomes should be planted at or just below soil surface — do not bury deep. In zone 9 plants bloom in April–May; in the fall before bloom, fertilize with a slow-release fertilizer formulated for acidic-soil plants. The moist to wet, acidic soil conditions of the Gulf South coastal plain are ideal — Louisiana irises grow naturally in bayous, swamp margins, and wet meadows.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, very acidic to slightly acidic, fertile soil — Louisiana irises thrive in the acid, wet soils that characterize the Gulf South coastal plain; Charjoy's Jan is a registered cultivar in the Louisiana iris series developed by Charles Chapman, producing the large, flat, fleur-de-lis-form flowers characteristic of the Louisiana iris hybrids (Iris x louisiana) — a complex group combining genetics from five native Gulf Coast iris species that together account for the extraordinary color range and form variation available in Louisiana iris breeding. Louisiana irises are among the most adapted irises for the Gulf South climate — heat tolerant through zone 9, fully cold hardy to zone 4, and thriving in the moist to wet, acidic soil conditions that are common across the Gulf Coast where bearded iris, Japanese iris, and Siberian iris perform poorly. The April–May bloom time fills a critical spring window when the large, brightly colored iris flowers are at their most dramatic — creating one of the finest native wildflower displays available for zone 9 water garden edges, bayou margins, and moist border plantings. Louisiana iris culture is supported by the Society for Louisiana Irises, a dedicated organization that maintains registration, hybridization, and evaluation of the hundreds of cultivars available.
sources: Society for Louisiana Irises — Iris x louisiana Hybridizing and Registration
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Louisiana Iris Culture for Gulf South
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Louisiana Iris for Florida Water Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Golden Emperor is a large-flowered exhibition chrysanthemum typically grown for cut flowers and fall display — the blooms can reach 4–5 inches across under optimal conditions. Pinch growing tips monthly from spring through early July to promote bushy plants with multiple flowering shoots. In zone 9 plants bloom October–November. Chrysanthemums are short-day plants — they initiate flowers when nights become longer than a critical threshold, making the fall bloom timing predictable and consistent regardless of when plants are pinched. Divide clumps each spring to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Golden Emperor Chrysanthemum performs best with consistent moisture and full sun for maximum flower size and color; Golden Emperor is a large-flowered chrysanthemum cultivar producing the classic double, globe-form flowers in a clean, rich golden-yellow color — the archetypal fall chrysanthemum color that has defined autumn display in American gardens and florist shops for decades. The large, fully double flowers (4–5 inches at their best under greenhouse or exhibition conditions) are composed of hundreds of tightly packed, inward-curving ray florets that create a spherical to slightly flattened globe form. As a short-day plant, chrysanthemum's bloom timing is controlled by day length rather than temperature or calendar date — flowering is triggered when nighttime darkness exceeds the plant's critical threshold, ensuring consistent fall blooming (September–November) regardless of growing conditions. In zone 9 chrysanthemums are less reliably perennial than in cooler zones, often performing as short-lived perennials that benefit from division and replanting every 2–3 years. The rich golden-yellow color of Golden Emperor provides the warm, classic autumnal display that is the most recognizable and widely desired chrysanthemum color for fall landscape and cut flower use.
sources: National Chrysanthemum Society — Exhibition Mum Cultivation Guide
University of Georgia Extension — Chrysanthemum morifolium for Southern Gardens
North Carolina State University Extension — Hardy Mum Varieties and Culture
